questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Surface (opencast) mining is generally the preferred method when the orebody is
options: deep-seated with a high stripping ratio
shallow and near-surface with a low stripping ratio
steeply dipping and very narrow at great depth
below a perennial water body
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Surface mining is economic only while the cost of removing overburden per tonne of ore (governed by the stripping ratio) stays below the saving over underground extraction.

**Reasoning.** A shallow, near-surface deposit needs little overburden removed per tonne, so the stripping ratio is low and surface mining is cheap and safe.

**Answer.** Shallow, near-surface deposit with a low stripping ratio.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Strip mining is most suitable for
options: thick, steeply inclined metalliferous lodes
flat-lying, tabular near-surface deposits such as coal and lignite
deep, irregular vein systems
alluvial placer gold deposits in river beds
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Strip mining removes overburden in long parallel strips and casts it into the adjacent mined-out void.

**Reasoning.** This geometry works only for flat-lying, tabular seams of roughly uniform thickness near the surface — typically coal and lignite.

**Answer.** Flat-lying tabular near-surface deposits (coal/lignite).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Contour mining is practised when a seam
options: lies flat under gently rolling plains
outcrops along the side of a hill, the cut following the contour
is buried more than 300 m below the surface
dips vertically into a deep basin
questions.solution:
**Concept.** In hilly terrain a seam outcrops along the hillside at a fairly constant elevation.

**Reasoning.** Contour mining follows that outcrop around the hill along the contour line, removing overburden from the cropline inward until the stripping ratio becomes uneconomic.

**Answer.** Seam outcropping along a hillside, cut following the contour.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Area (open-cast strip) mining over flat terrain proceeds by
options: sinking a vertical shaft and driving levels
mining a series of long parallel strips, spoil cast into the previous strip
excavating a single circular pit that widens with depth
driving inclined drifts from a central decline
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Area mining is the flat-terrain form of strip mining.

**Reasoning.** The orebody is worked in successive parallel strips; overburden from the active strip is cast into the void left by the previous strip, minimising rehandling.

**Answer.** Successive parallel strips with spoil cast into the prior strip.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: In a single-seam dragline strip operation, the dragline is employed principally to
options: load coal directly into railway wagons
remove the overburden and cast it into the de-coaled (mined-out) void
drill the production blastholes
transport coal to the surface stockpile
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The walking dragline is an overburden-stripping machine, not a coal-loading machine.

**Reasoning.** It sits on the highwall side, digs the blasted overburden and casts (sidecasts) it into the adjacent void from which coal has already been removed.

**Answer.** Removes overburden and casts it into the de-coaled void.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: An opencast mine removes 8 Mt of waste to extract 2 Mt of ore. The overall stripping ratio (t waste per t ore) is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stripping ratio $SR=\dfrac{\text{waste}}{\text{ore}}$.

**Working.**
$$SR=\frac{8}{2}=4$$

**Answer.** 4 t waste per t ore.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: In an opencast pit, the 'highwall' is
options: the spoil side where overburden is dumped
the unexcavated working face/wall on the solid (un-mined) side
the haul-road ramp to the surface
the floor of the lowest bench
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A strip cut has two walls: highwall and lowwall.

**Reasoning.** The highwall is the exposed, unexcavated face on the solid side that advances as mining proceeds; the lowwall (spoil side) faces the dumped overburden.

**Answer.** The unexcavated working face on the solid side.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A pit is mined to 90 m depth in benches of 15 m height. The number of benches is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Number of benches $=\dfrac{\text{pit depth}}{\text{bench height}}$.

**Working.**
$$n=\frac{90}{15}=6$$

**Answer.** 6 benches.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A catch berm (safety bench) is provided on a pit slope mainly to
options: increase the overall pit slope angle
arrest rolling/falling rock and improve overall slope stability
store blasted ore temporarily
act as the main haul road
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Berms break a tall slope into segments.

**Reasoning.** A catch berm intercepts loose rock that ravels off the bench faces and, by flattening the overall slope, raises the factor of safety.

**Answer.** To catch falling rock and improve slope stability.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A bench face rises 12 m vertically over a horizontal run of 6 m. The bench face angle, in degrees, is ____ (give to two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Face angle $\theta=\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{rise}}{\text{run}}\right)$.

**Working.**
$$\theta=\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{12}{6}\right)=\tan^{-1}(2)=63.43^{\circ}$$

**Answer.** $63.43^{\circ}$.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: In a shovel-dumper (truck) combination, the shovel
options: hauls material to the dump
excavates and loads material into the dumpers
spreads spoil on the dump
drills the blastholes
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The shovel-truck system separates digging/loading from hauling.

**Reasoning.** The shovel is the excavating-loading unit; trucks (dumpers) are the discontinuous haulage units that carry the load to the dump or crusher.

**Answer.** Excavates and loads material into the dumpers.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: 1000 m³ of rock (bank volume) swells by 25% on excavation. The loose (bulked) volume, in m³, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Loose volume $=$ bank volume $\times(1+\text{swell})$.

**Working.**
$$V_L=1000\,(1+0.25)=1250\ \text{m}^3$$

**Answer.** 1250 m³.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Rock swells by 25% on digging. The load (shrinkage) factor that converts loose volume back to bank volume is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Load factor $=\dfrac{1}{1+\text{swell}}=\dfrac{\text{bank}}{\text{loose}}$.

**Working.**
$$LF=\frac{1}{1.25}=0.80$$

**Answer.** 0.80.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The swell (bulking) factor of a rock accounts for the fact that
options: rock compacts when blasted
the loose excavated rock occupies more volume than it did in place
the rock loses mass during excavation
the truck bed volume changes with temperature
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Breaking rock creates voids between fragments.

**Reasoning.** The broken (loose) material therefore occupies a greater volume than the same mass did in the bank; this volume increase is the swell or bulking.

**Answer.** Loose rock occupies more volume than in place.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Rock has a bank (in-situ) density of 2.5 t/m³ and a loose density of 2.0 t/m³. The load factor (loose-to-bank volume conversion) is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Load factor $=\dfrac{\rho_{loose}}{\rho_{bank}}$ (mass conserved).

**Working.**
$$LF=\frac{2.0}{2.5}=0.80$$

**Answer.** 0.80.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A bench block has an in-situ volume of 1000 m³ and the rock density is 2.5 t/m³. The tonnage, in t, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tonnage $=$ in-situ volume $\times$ bank density.

**Working.**
$$T=1000\times2.5=2500\ \text{t}$$

**Answer.** 2500 t.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A bucket wheel excavator (BWE) is best suited to
options: blasted hard, abrasive granite
continuous excavation of soft, unconsolidated overburden
selective mining of narrow high-grade veins
underwater dredging of placer sands
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The BWE is a continuous, high-capacity digging machine with limited cutting force.

**Reasoning.** It excels in soft, free-digging overburden (lignite mines) where no drilling/blasting is needed, feeding a continuous conveyor; it cannot dig hard rock.

**Answer.** Continuous excavation of soft, unconsolidated overburden.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A surface miner is used to
options: drill and charge production holes
cut, crush and load mineral in one pass without drilling and blasting
pump water out of the pit
build and maintain haul roads only
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The surface miner is a continuous cutting machine with a rotating drum.

**Reasoning.** It mills the rock, sizes it and (optionally) loads it onto trucks/conveyor in a single operation, eliminating drilling and blasting in moderately hard, bedded deposits.

**Answer.** Cuts, crushes and loads mineral without drilling and blasting.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A working bench must accommodate a shovel of working width 10 m, a dumper of width 6 m, and a safety clearance of 4 m. The minimum bench width, in m, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Minimum operating bench width is the sum of equipment footprints plus clearance.

**Working.**
$$W=10+6+4=20\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 20 m.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: In a surface mine, a bulldozer (dozer) is typically used for
options: long-distance haulage of ore
ripping, dozing, grade preparation and spreading spoil on dumps
primary excavation of hard rock benches
loading ore into railway wagons
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The dozer is a versatile support (ancillary) machine.

**Reasoning.** Fitted with a ripper and blade, it loosens soft strata, pushes material short distances, prepares grades and spreads dumped spoil; it is not a primary loader/hauler.

**Answer.** Ripping, dozing, grade preparation and dump spreading.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A wheeled scraper is most economical for
options: digging hard, blasted rock
loading, hauling and spreading loose material over short to medium distances
deep vertical excavation
underground development headings
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A scraper loads, transports and discharges material in a single unit.

**Reasoning.** It is efficient in soft, free-digging ground over short-to-medium hauls, e.g. topsoil and soft overburden removal; it cannot handle hard rock.

**Answer.** Loading, hauling and spreading loose material over short/medium distances.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A bench block is 200 m long, 30 m wide and 12 m high. Its in-situ volume, in m³, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Block volume $=L\times W\times H$.

**Working.**
$$V=200\times30\times12=72{,}000\ \text{m}^3$$

**Answer.** 72,000 m³.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The bench block of the previous problem (72,000 m³) has a rock density of 2.5 t/m³. Its tonnage, in t, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Tonnage $=$ volume $\times$ density.

**Working.**
$$T=72{,}000\times2.5=180{,}000\ \text{t}$$

**Answer.** 180,000 t.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The angle of repose of a spoil dump is
options: the angle of the haul-road gradient
the maximum natural slope angle the loose material can stand at
the dip of the underlying seam
the bench face angle of the highwall
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Loose granular material piles up to a limiting slope.

**Reasoning.** The angle of repose is the steepest angle at which the dumped material is stable without sliding; it controls dump height and footprint.

**Answer.** Maximum natural slope angle of the loose material.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: An external spoil dump covers a base area of 50,000 m² to an average height of 20 m. Its loose storage capacity, in m³, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Capacity $\approx$ base area $\times$ average height.

**Working.**
$$V=50{,}000\times20=1{,}000{,}000\ \text{m}^3$$

**Answer.** 1,000,000 m³.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The main benefit of in-pit crushing and conveying (IPCC) over an all-truck system is
options: elimination of blasting
reduced truck haulage distance/cost and lower diesel and tyre consumption
increased bench face angle
higher dilution of ore
questions.solution:
**Concept.** IPCC brings the crusher to the pit and conveys crushed rock out.

**Reasoning.** Trucks need only make a short haul to the in-pit crusher; the belt does the long, deep lift. This cuts diesel, tyre and maintenance costs and truck fleet size.

**Answer.** Reduced truck haulage distance and cost.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A bench advances 5 m per shift across a 30 m width to a height of 12 m. The in-situ output per shift, in m³, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Output $=$ advance $\times$ width $\times$ height.

**Working.**
$$V=5\times30\times12=1800\ \text{m}^3/\text{shift}$$

**Answer.** 1800 m³.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Terrace mining is characterised by
options: a single deep face worked from the bottom up
several horizontal benches (terraces) worked simultaneously down the deposit
casting all overburden by dragline only
underground extraction below a terraced hill
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Thick deposits are split into multiple benches.

**Reasoning.** In terrace mining a number of horizontal benches are developed and worked concurrently, each with its own equipment, allowing high output from thick overburden/ore sequences.

**Answer.** Several horizontal benches worked simultaneously.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A truck's full cycle time (load + haul + dump + return + spot) is 20 min and its loading time at the shovel is 4 min. The number of trucks needed to keep one shovel continuously busy is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Match factor: trucks per shovel $=\dfrac{\text{truck cycle time}}{\text{loading time}}$.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{20}{4}=5$$

**Answer.** 5 trucks.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Whether a rock can be ripped (rather than blasted) is most commonly judged from its
options: colour and lustre
seismic (P-wave) velocity
specific gravity alone
moisture content
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rippability correlates with intactness/strength, indexed by seismic velocity.

**Reasoning.** Low P-wave velocity indicates weak, fractured ground that can be ripped; high velocity indicates massive, strong rock requiring blasting. Manufacturers publish rippability charts versus seismic velocity.

**Answer.** Seismic (P-wave) velocity.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A rope shovel has a dipper of rated (struck) capacity 5 m³ and works at a fill factor of 0.9. The loose volume excavated per dipper pass, in m³, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Actual loose load per pass $=$ rated capacity $\times$ fill factor.

**Working.**
$$V=5\times0.9=4.5\ \text{m}^3$$

**Answer.** 4.5 m³ (loose).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The shovel completes one dig-swing-dump-return cycle in 30 s. The number of cycles per hour is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Cycles per hour $=\dfrac{3600}{\text{cycle time (s)}}$.

**Working.**
$$n=\frac{3600}{30}=120$$

**Answer.** 120 cycles/h.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: With 4.5 m³ loose per pass and 120 cycles per hour, the loose output, in m³/h, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Loose hourly output $=$ load per pass $\times$ cycles per hour.

**Working.**
$$Q_L=4.5\times120=540\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Answer.** 540 m³/h (loose).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The rock swells by 25% on digging. The in-situ (bank) output of the shovel, corresponding to 540 m³/h loose, in m³/h, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Bank volume $=$ loose volume $\times$ load factor $=\dfrac{\text{loose}}{1+\text{swell}}$.

**Working.**
$$Q_B=540\times\frac{1}{1.25}=540\times0.8=432\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Answer.** 432 m³/h (bank).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A dumper holds 18 m³ (loose) and the shovel delivers 4.5 m³ loose per pass. The number of dipper passes to fill the dumper is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Passes $=\dfrac{\text{truck loose capacity}}{\text{loose load per pass}}$.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{18}{4.5}=4$$

**Answer.** 4 passes.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: With 4 dipper passes at 30 s per pass, the truck loading time, in seconds, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Loading time $=$ passes $\times$ cycle time.

**Working.**
$$t_L=4\times30=120\ \text{s}$$

**Answer.** 120 s (2 min).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The dipper (bucket) fill factor is usually less than unity because
options: the bucket is deliberately made oversize
the bucket seldom fills to its rated struck capacity owing to bulking and digging conditions
the rock loses mass during digging
the operator always overloads the bucket
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rated (struck) capacity assumes a perfectly filled, level bucket.

**Reasoning.** In practice, fragmentation, bulking, diggability and operator skill leave the bucket partly empty, so the actual load is a fraction (the fill factor) of rated capacity.

**Answer.** The bucket seldom fills to its rated capacity.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A hydraulic excavator in backhoe configuration digs
options: above the track level into the face
below the track level, pulling material toward the machine
only horizontally at track level
vertically upward only
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Backhoe versus face-shovel geometry.

**Reasoning.** A backhoe bucket curls inward and downward, excavating below the machine's standing level — ideal for digging trenches, sumps and loading from a lower bench.

**Answer.** Below track level, toward the machine.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A front (face) shovel is designed to dig
options: below the machine, toward itself
above track level, crowding into the bench face
only on flat ground at grade
underwater material only
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A face shovel crowds the bucket outward and upward.

**Reasoning.** It excavates material above its standing level by thrusting into the bench face, making it suited to loading from a face higher than the machine.

**Answer.** Above track level, into the face.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A walking dragline has a 40 m³ bucket and works at a fill factor of 0.85. The loose volume per cycle, in m³, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Loose load $=$ bucket capacity $\times$ fill factor.

**Working.**
$$V=40\times0.85=34\ \text{m}^3$$

**Answer.** 34 m³ (loose).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The dragline cycle time is 60 s. The number of cycles per hour is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Cycles/h $=\dfrac{3600}{\text{cycle time}}$.

**Working.**
$$n=\frac{3600}{60}=60$$

**Answer.** 60 cycles/h.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: With 34 m³ loose per cycle and 60 cycles per hour, the dragline's loose output, in m³/h, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Loose hourly output $=$ load per cycle $\times$ cycles/h.

**Working.**
$$Q_L=34\times60=2040\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Answer.** 2040 m³/h (loose).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The overburden swells by 25%. The dragline's in-situ (bank) output corresponding to 2040 m³/h loose, in m³/h, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Bank output $=$ loose output $\times\dfrac{1}{1+\text{swell}}$.

**Working.**
$$Q_B=2040\times0.8=1632\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Answer.** 1632 m³/h (bank).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A walking dragline propels itself by
options: crawler tracks like a shovel
walking shoes/cams, resting on a circular tub while digging
rubber-tyred wheels
rail bogies on a temporary track
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Draglines are too heavy for crawlers; ground bearing pressure must be very low.

**Reasoning.** The machine rests on a large circular tub (low bearing pressure) while digging, and 'walks' short distances on a pair of eccentric-driven shoes to reposition.

**Answer.** Walking shoes, resting on a tub while digging.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The 'dumping radius' of a dragline is
options: the vertical reach above the tub
the horizontal distance from the centre of rotation to the point of spoil discharge
the maximum depth it can dig below the tub
the radius of the bucket teeth
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Dragline range is defined by digging reach, dig depth and dumping radius/height.

**Reasoning.** The dumping radius is the horizontal distance from the rotation axis at which spoil can be discharged; together with dumping height it must clear the spoil pile.

**Answer.** Horizontal distance from rotation centre to spoil discharge point.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The 'digging depth' of a dragline is
options: the maximum depth below the tub level it can excavate
the height of the boom point
the swing angle per cycle
the bucket fill factor
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A dragline casts its bucket out and drags it back, excavating below its standing level.

**Reasoning.** Digging depth is the greatest depth below the tub that the dragged bucket can reach; it limits the overburden thickness a dragline can strip in one pass.

**Answer.** Maximum depth below the tub it can excavate.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A bucket wheel excavator has 12 buckets on the wheel, which rotates at 4 rev/min. The number of bucket discharges per minute is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Discharges/min $=$ buckets on wheel $\times$ wheel speed (rev/min).

**Working.**
$$d=12\times4=48\ \text{discharges/min}$$

**Answer.** 48.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Each BWE bucket discharges 0.5 m³ (loose). With 48 discharges per minute, the theoretical loose output, in m³/min, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Output $=$ discharges/min $\times$ bucket capacity.

**Working.**
$$Q=48\times0.5=24\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Answer.** 24 m³/min.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Converting 24 m³/min to an hourly rate gives ____ m³/h (loose).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Multiply per-minute rate by 60.

**Working.**
$$Q=24\times60=1440\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Answer.** 1440 m³/h.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A dozer is often assigned to 'push-load' a fleet of scrapers in order to
options: increase the scraper's haul speed
shorten the scraper loading time and improve bowl fill
reduce tyre wear on the scraper
eliminate the need for a haul road
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Scrapers struggle to self-load in firmer ground.

**Reasoning.** A pusher dozer adds tractive force during loading, filling the bowl faster and more fully, which raises scraper productivity in the load phase.

**Answer.** Shorten loading time and improve bowl fill.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A dozer moves 3 m³ (loose) of material per 0.5-min push-return cycle. Its loose production, in m³/h, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Production $=\dfrac{\text{load per cycle}}{\text{cycle time}}$.

**Working.**
$$Q=\frac{3}{0.5}=6\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}=6\times60=360\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Answer.** 360 m³/h (loose).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The mechanical availability of an excavating machine is
options: the fraction of scheduled time during which it is mechanically fit to operate
the fraction of its rated capacity actually loaded
the ratio of bank volume to loose volume
the number of cycles per hour
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Availability isolates mechanical reliability from operating practice.

**Reasoning.** It is the proportion of scheduled time the machine is up (not under repair). Utilization then measures how much of that up-time is actually used.

**Answer.** Fraction of scheduled time it is fit to operate.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A machine is scheduled for 16 h but is down for repair for 4 h. Its mechanical availability is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Availability $=\dfrac{\text{scheduled}-\text{downtime}}{\text{scheduled}}$.

**Working.**
$$A=\frac{16-4}{16}=\frac{12}{16}=0.75$$

**Answer.** 0.75.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Of 12 h available, the machine actually operates for 9 h. Its utilization of availability is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Utilization $=\dfrac{\text{operating time}}{\text{available time}}$.

**Working.**
$$U=\frac{9}{12}=0.75$$

**Answer.** 0.75.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: If the machine operates 9 h out of a 16-h scheduled shift, its overall use-of-time factor is ____ (four decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Use-of-time $=\dfrac{\text{operating}}{\text{scheduled}}=A\times U$.

**Working.**
$$\frac{9}{16}=0.5625\quad(=0.75\times0.75)$$

**Answer.** 0.5625.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: 'Utilization of availability' measures
options: the fraction of available (up) time during which the machine actually operates
the fraction of scheduled time lost to repairs
the bucket fill factor
the swell of the excavated rock
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Once a machine is available, it may still stand idle (no trucks, operator break, etc.).

**Reasoning.** Utilization of availability captures how much of the up-time is converted into productive operating time.

**Answer.** Fraction of up-time actually operated.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A shovel's theoretical bank output is 400 m³/h. With availability 0.8 and utilization 0.75, its effective bank output, in m³/h, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Effective output $=$ theoretical $\times$ availability $\times$ utilization.

**Working.**
$$Q_{eff}=400\times0.8\times0.75=240\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Answer.** 240 m³/h.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: At an effective output of 240 m³/h over a 12-h operating shift, the bank volume produced, in m³, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Shift output $=$ effective rate $\times$ operating hours.

**Working.**
$$V=240\times12=2880\ \text{m}^3$$

**Answer.** 2880 m³.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A pit must deliver 720 m³/h (bank). If each shovel gives an effective 240 m³/h, the number of shovels required is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Number of units $=\dfrac{\text{required output}}{\text{output per unit}}$.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{720}{240}=3$$

**Answer.** 3 shovels.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A truck-shovel fleet whose match factor (MF) equals 1.0 indicates that
options: trucks are over-supplied and queue at the shovel
the fleet is balanced — neither the trucks nor the shovel waits on the other
the shovel is idle most of the time
the haul road is too steep
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $MF=\dfrac{\text{truck arrival rate}}{\text{shovel service rate}}$.

**Reasoning.** $MF=1$ means trucks arrive exactly as fast as the shovel can load them: a balanced fleet with minimal queuing and minimal shovel idle time. $MF<1$ underutilises the shovel; $MF>1$ queues trucks.

**Answer.** Balanced fleet, neither resource waiting.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A loaded dumper travels 2.5 km to the dump at 30 km/h. The loaded haul time, in minutes, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Time $=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}}$.

**Working.**
$$t=\frac{2.5}{30}\text{ h}=\frac{2.5}{30}\times60=5\ \text{min}$$

**Answer.** 5 min.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The empty dumper returns 2.5 km at 50 km/h. The return (empty) travel time, in minutes, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $t=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}}$.

**Working.**
$$t=\frac{2.5}{50}\times60=3\ \text{min}$$

**Answer.** 3 min.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A dumper's cycle is: loading 2 min, loaded haul 5 min, dumping 1 min, empty return 3 min, spotting 1 min. The total cycle time, in minutes, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Cycle time is the sum of all phase times.

**Working.**
$$t_c=2+5+1+3+1=12\ \text{min}$$

**Answer.** 12 min.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: With a 12-min cycle, the number of trips a dumper makes per hour is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Trips/h $=\dfrac{60}{\text{cycle time (min)}}$.

**Working.**
$$n=\frac{60}{12}=5$$

**Answer.** 5 trips/h.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Each dumper carries a payload of 50 t and makes 5 trips per hour. Its hourly haulage, in t/h, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Output $=$ payload $\times$ trips/h.

**Working.**
$$Q=50\times5=250\ \text{t/h}$$

**Answer.** 250 t/h.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A target of 750 t/h is required. With each dumper hauling 250 t/h, the number of dumpers needed is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Fleet $=\dfrac{\text{required}}{\text{per truck}}$.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{750}{250}=3$$

**Answer.** 3 dumpers.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: For a fixed shovel, replacing the truck fleet with fewer but larger-payload trucks generally
options: reduces the number of trucks but lengthens each loading time (more passes)
eliminates the need for a shovel
reduces the bucket fill factor to zero
always reduces total system cost regardless of haul distance
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Truck and shovel must be size-matched.

**Reasoning.** Bigger trucks need fewer units for the same tonnage, but each takes more dipper passes to fill, increasing loading time and possibly shovel waiting. An ideal match uses 3–5 passes per truck.

**Answer.** Fewer trucks but longer loading per truck.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Loading time per truck is 2 min and the truck cycle time is 12 min. The number of trucks giving a match factor of exactly 1.0 is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $MF=\dfrac{N\times t_{load}}{t_{cycle}}$; set $MF=1$.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{t_{cycle}}{t_{load}}=\frac{12}{2}=6$$

**Answer.** 6 trucks.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: If the match factor of a truck-shovel fleet exceeds 1.0, then
options: the shovel is starved and stands idle
trucks arrive faster than the shovel can load them and queue up
the trucks travel faster than rated
the haul road gradient is negative
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $MF>1$ means truck-loading demand exceeds shovel service capacity.

**Reasoning.** Trucks accumulate at the shovel forming a queue; the shovel becomes the bottleneck and trucks lose time waiting.

**Answer.** Trucks queue at the saturated shovel.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: With only 4 trucks (loading time 2 min, cycle 12 min), the match factor is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $MF=\dfrac{N\times t_{load}}{t_{cycle}}$.

**Working.**
$$MF=\frac{4\times2}{12}=\frac{8}{12}=0.67$$

**Answer.** 0.67 (shovel under-utilised).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The overall stripping ratio of an opencast mine is
options: the waste-to-ore ratio for the next incremental push only
the total waste divided by the total ore over the mine life
the bench face angle divided by the overall slope angle
the ratio of loose to bank volume
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Several stripping ratios are defined.

**Reasoning.** The overall (average) SR is the ratio of all waste to be moved to all ore to be recovered over the entire life of the pit; it sizes the total waste task.

**Answer.** Total waste / total ore over mine life.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The instantaneous (incremental) stripping ratio refers to
options: the waste-to-ore ratio for a specific pushback or increment of the pit
the lifetime average waste-to-ore ratio
the maximum economic stripping ratio
the ratio of berm width to bench height
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Incremental SR is evaluated push-by-push.

**Reasoning.** It is the waste removed to expose the ore in one particular increment (cut/pushback); it rises as the pit deepens and is compared against the break-even SR for that increment.

**Answer.** Waste/ore for a specific increment of the pit.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Recoverable value of ore is Rs 1000/t, ore production cost is Rs 400/t, and waste stripping cost is Rs 120 per tonne of waste. The break-even stripping ratio (t waste per t ore) is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $BESR=\dfrac{\text{ore value}-\text{ore production cost}}{\text{stripping cost per tonne waste}}$.

**Working.**
$$BESR=\frac{1000-400}{120}=\frac{600}{120}=5$$

**Answer.** 5 t waste per t ore.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Surface mining of an increment remains economic as long as its
options: actual stripping ratio is less than or equal to the break-even stripping ratio
actual stripping ratio exceeds the break-even stripping ratio
bench height equals the berm width
overall slope angle exceeds the face angle
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The pit limit is set where actual SR equals BESR.

**Reasoning.** While the waste-to-ore ratio of the next increment is at or below the break-even value, the increment pays its way; once it exceeds BESR, further surface mining loses money and defines the ultimate pit limit.

**Answer.** Actual SR $\le$ BESR.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A pit with an overall stripping ratio of 3 will recover 10 Mt of ore. The waste to be removed, in Mt, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Waste $=SR\times$ ore.

**Working.**
$$W=3\times10=30\ \text{Mt}$$

**Answer.** 30 Mt.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: For 10 Mt ore and 30 Mt waste, the total material to be excavated, in Mt, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total moved $=$ ore $+$ waste.

**Working.**
$$M=10+30=40\ \text{Mt}$$

**Answer.** 40 Mt.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: In the bench profile shown, each bench is 12 m high with a 60° face angle and a 4 m wide berm. The overall pit slope angle, in degrees, is ____ (give to one decimal).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='0' y='0' width='360' height='220' fill='#ffffff'/><polyline points='20,30 120,30 140,90 220,90 240,150 320,150 340,210 350,210' fill='none' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='20' y1='30' x2='340' y2='210' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='5 4'/><text x='150' y='66' font-size='12' fill='#111111'>H = 12 m</text><text x='160' y='86' font-size='11' fill='#111111'>berm B = 4 m</text><text x='40' y='48' font-size='11' fill='#111111'>face = 60°</text><text x='250' y='200' font-size='11' fill='#dc2626'>overall slope</text></svg>
figure.caption: Bench profile: bench height H, berm width B, face angle; dashed line is the overall pit slope.

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The overall slope joins the crest to the toe; per bench the horizontal advance is the face projection plus the berm.

**Working.**
$$\text{horizontal/bench}=\frac{H}{\tan 60^{\circ}}+B=\frac{12}{1.732}+4=6.93+4=10.93\ \text{m}$$
$$\theta_{overall}=\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{12}{10.93}\right)=\tan^{-1}(1.098)=47.7^{\circ}$$

**Answer.** $47.7^{\circ}$.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A 60 m deep pit is worked in 12 m benches. The number of berms (catch benches) between crest and floor is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Benches $=\dfrac{\text{depth}}{\text{bench height}}$; berms $=$ benches $-1$.

**Working.**
$$\text{benches}=\frac{60}{12}=5,\quad \text{berms}=5-1=4$$

**Answer.** 4 berms.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Using an overall slope angle of 47.7°, the horizontal offset from crest to toe for a 60 m deep pit, in m, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Offset $=\dfrac{\text{depth}}{\tan(\text{overall slope})}$.

**Working.**
$$x=\frac{60}{\tan 47.7^{\circ}}=\frac{60}{1.098}=54.6\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 54.6 m.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Flattening (reducing) the overall pit slope angle generally
options: reduces waste stripping but lowers slope stability
increases waste stripping but improves slope stability
has no effect on either waste or stability
increases ore grade
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Slope angle trades stripping cost against stability.

**Reasoning.** A flatter wall pushes the crest back, exposing more waste to be removed (higher SR), but the lower, less-steep wall has a higher factor of safety.

**Answer.** More waste but greater stability.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A belt conveyor carries material of cross-sectional area 0.2 m² at a belt speed of 2.5 m/s; the loose bulk density is 1.6 t/m³. Its capacity, in t/h, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $Q=3600\,A\,v\,\rho$ (with $A$ in m², $v$ in m/s, $\rho$ in t/m³).

**Working.**
$$Q=3600\times0.2\times2.5\times1.6=2880\ \text{t/h}$$

**Answer.** 2880 t/h.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: If the belt speed is doubled to 5 m/s (same load cross-section and density), the conveyor capacity, in t/h, becomes ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Capacity is directly proportional to belt speed.

**Working.**
$$Q=2880\times\frac{5}{2.5}=5760\ \text{t/h}$$

**Answer.** 5760 t/h.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Belt conveyors are preferred over trucks for waste/ore haulage when the requirement is
options: flexible, frequently changing haul routes
continuous, high-volume transport up steep grades on a fixed route
very small intermittent tonnages
selective transport of individual large boulders
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Conveyors are continuous, fixed-path systems.

**Reasoning.** They move large, steady tonnages economically, can climb grades far steeper than truck ramps, and have low energy/labour cost per tonne — provided the route is fixed.

**Answer.** Continuous high-volume haulage up steep grades on a fixed route.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A conveyor lifts 2880 t/h of material through a vertical height of 20 m. Ignoring friction and taking g = 9.81 m/s², the lifting power, in kW, is ____ (nearest whole number).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Lift power $=\dot m\,g\,h$, with mass flow in kg/s.

**Working.**
$$\dot m=\frac{2880\times1000}{3600}=800\ \text{kg/s}$$
$$P=800\times9.81\times20=156960\ \text{W}\approx157\ \text{kW}$$

**Answer.** 157 kW.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Topsoil is stripped and stockpiled separately before mining so that
options: it can be sold as construction aggregate
it can be respread during reclamation to support revegetation
it increases the ore grade
it reduces the swell factor of the overburden
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Topsoil carries the nutrients, seeds and microbes needed for plant growth.

**Reasoning.** Preserving it separately allows it to be replaced over the regraded spoil during reclamation, enabling successful revegetation and land restoration.

**Answer.** Respread during reclamation to support revegetation.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: 1,000,000 bank m³ of waste, swelling by 25%, is placed on an external dump. The loose dump volume, in m³, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Loose volume $=$ bank volume $\times(1+\text{swell})$.

**Working.**
$$V_L=1{,}000{,}000\times1.25=1{,}250{,}000\ \text{m}^3$$

**Answer.** 1,250,000 m³.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The stability of a large external spoil dump is improved by
options: increasing its overall slope angle as much as possible
benching/terracing the dump and limiting its overall slope angle
placing the wettest material at the toe
removing all internal drainage
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Dump stability follows the same slope principles as pit walls.

**Reasoning.** Terracing breaks the height into stable lifts, flattening the overall slope toward the angle of repose; good foundation preparation and drainage prevent pore-pressure-driven failure.

**Answer.** Terrace the dump and limit overall slope angle.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A dump of 1,250,000 m³ loose volume is built to an average height of 25 m. Its base footprint area, in m², is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Area $=\dfrac{\text{volume}}{\text{average height}}$.

**Working.**
$$A=\frac{1{,}250{,}000}{25}=50{,}000\ \text{m}^2$$

**Answer.** 50,000 m².

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A shovel's combined owning + operating cost is Rs 30,000 per hour and it produces an effective 240 bank m³/h. Its unit excavation cost, in Rs per bank m³, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Unit cost $=\dfrac{\text{hourly cost}}{\text{hourly output}}$.

**Working.**
$$c=\frac{30000}{240}=125\ \text{Rs/m}^3$$

**Answer.** Rs 125 per bank m³.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Surface mining usually achieves higher output per man-shift than underground mining mainly because
options: the ore is always richer near the surface
very large equipment can be used with few size, ventilation or roof-support constraints
no drilling and blasting is ever needed
haul distances are always shorter
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Productivity scales with equipment size and freedom of operation.

**Reasoning.** Open workings impose no roof-span, ventilation or gallery-size limits, so very large shovels, draglines and trucks can be deployed, giving much higher output per worker than confined underground operations.

**Answer.** Large equipment usable with few size/ventilation constraints.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Highwall mining is a method that
options: removes the entire highwall by blasting
extracts coal from an exposed highwall using a remotely operated continuous miner/auger driven into the seam
builds a retaining wall on the highwall
mines only the spoil dump
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Highwall mining recovers reserves left beyond the economic stripping limit of an opencast pit.

**Reasoning.** A remotely operated continuous miner or auger is driven horizontally into the coal seam exposed in the final highwall, recovering coal in a series of parallel entries without removing further overburden.

**Answer.** Remotely extracts coal from the exposed highwall seam.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: An auger miner recovers coal by
options: pumping coal slurry from a borehole
boring a series of parallel holes into the seam exposed in the highwall
blasting the highwall and loading with a shovel
dredging coal from a flooded pit
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Augering is the simplest form of highwall extraction.

**Reasoning.** Large-diameter augers bore horizontally into the exposed seam, the flights conveying broken coal back to the bench; a web of coal is left between holes for support.

**Answer.** Boring parallel holes into the highwall seam.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A loaded dumper has a gross weight of 100 t and operates where the rolling resistance is 2% of gross weight. Taking g = 9.81 m/s², the rolling resistance force, in kN, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rolling resistance force $=RR\%\times W g$.

**Working.**
$$F_{RR}=0.02\times100{,}000\times9.81=19620\ \text{N}=19.62\ \text{kN}$$

**Answer.** 19.62 kN.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The same 100 t dumper climbs an 8% grade. The grade resistance force (using the small-angle approximation), in kN, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Grade resistance $\approx$ grade(fraction)$\times W g$.

**Working.**
$$F_{GR}=0.08\times100{,}000\times9.81=78480\ \text{N}=78.48\ \text{kN}$$

**Answer.** 78.48 kN.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Combining a 19.62 kN rolling resistance with a 78.48 kN grade resistance, the total resistance the dumper must overcome, in kN, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total resistance $=F_{RR}+F_{GR}$.

**Working.**
$$F=19.62+78.48=98.10\ \text{kN}$$

**Answer.** 98.1 kN.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The dumper must develop a rimpull equal to the total resistance of 98.1 kN while climbing at 10 km/h. The power required at the wheels, in kW, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Power $=$ force $\times$ speed (consistent units).

**Working.**
$$v=\frac{10}{3.6}=2.778\ \text{m/s}$$
$$P=98100\times2.778=272{,}500\ \text{W}=272.5\ \text{kW}$$

**Answer.** 272.5 kW.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The 'effective grade' (total resistance expressed as an equivalent grade) experienced by a haul truck is
options: grade resistance minus rolling resistance
grade percentage plus rolling-resistance percentage
the bench face angle
always equal to the rolling resistance alone
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rolling resistance can be expressed as an equivalent grade.

**Reasoning.** The truck's engine sees the sum of the actual climb and the rolling drag; adding the two percentages gives one effective grade against which rimpull/speed are read from performance charts.

**Answer.** Grade % + rolling-resistance %.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: On an 8% grade with 2% rolling resistance, the effective grade, in %, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Effective grade $=$ grade % $+$ RR %.

**Working.**
$$EG=8+2=10\%$$

**Answer.** 10%.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A retarder on a haul truck is used to
options: increase engine power on upgrades
control and limit truck speed on downgrades without overheating the service brakes
raise the dump body faster
improve tyre traction in mud
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Descending a loaded truck releases large gravitational energy.

**Reasoning.** A retarder (hydraulic/electric/engine) dissipates this energy to hold a safe steady speed on long downgrades, sparing the friction service brakes from overheating.

**Answer.** Controls speed on downgrades.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Slope stability radar (SSR) on a pit wall is used to
options: measure ore grade in the wall
monitor real-time wall movement and give early warning of impending failure
detect methane in the highwall
survey the pit topography for volume
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Wall failures are preceded by accelerating displacement.

**Reasoning.** Ground-based radar scans the wall continuously and detects sub-millimetre movement over large areas, allowing alarms to be raised and the area evacuated before collapse.

**Answer.** Real-time movement monitoring / early warning.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A continuous surface mining system (excavator–conveyor–spreader) differs from a shovel–truck system mainly in that
options: it handles material as a continuous flow rather than discrete truck loads
it cannot move overburden
it requires drilling and blasting of every bench
it works only in hard, abrasive rock
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Continuous systems link digging, transport and dumping in an uninterrupted chain.

**Reasoning.** A bucket-wheel excavator feeds a belt conveyor that feeds a spreader, moving material as a steady stream — eliminating the cyclic load/haul/dump pattern of shovel-truck operation. It suits soft, free-digging ground.

**Answer.** Continuous material flow rather than discrete loads.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: In a continuous overburden system, the spreader
options: excavates the overburden from the face
places (stacks) the conveyed overburden on the spoil-side dump
crushes the ore
drives the conveyor belt
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The spreader is the dumping end of the continuous chain.

**Reasoning.** It receives overburden from the conveyor and discharges it via a boom to build the internal/external spoil dump in controlled lifts.

**Answer.** Stacks conveyed overburden on the spoil dump.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A BWE-conveyor-spreader system sustains 1440 m³/h and operates 20 h per day. Its daily output, in m³, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Daily output $=$ hourly rate $\times$ operating hours.

**Working.**
$$V=1440\times20=28{,}800\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Answer.** 28,800 m³/day.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Which of the following are continuous (rather than cyclic) excavating machines?
options: Bucket wheel excavator
Surface miner
Rope (cable) shovel
Bucket chain excavator
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Continuous machines dig without a discrete load-swing-dump cycle.

**Reasoning.** The bucket wheel excavator, surface miner and bucket chain excavator all excavate in a continuous stream. A rope shovel is the classic cyclic (discontinuous) digger.

**Answer.** BWE, surface miner and bucket chain excavator.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The ultimate pit limit of an opencast mine defines
options: the final pit outline that maximises total profit, its boundary set where SR equals BESR
the depth of the first bench only
the angle of repose of the dump
the width of the haul road
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The pit is expanded only while each increment is profitable.

**Reasoning.** The ultimate (final) pit is the largest outline for which every boundary increment still satisfies actual SR $\le$ BESR; expanding further would add waste worth more than the ore it exposes.

**Answer.** Profit-maximising final outline (boundary at SR = BESR).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Phased (pushback / cutback) mining within the ultimate pit is practised mainly to
options: increase the overall stripping ratio
defer waste removal and expose ore earlier, improving early cash flow and NPV
reduce the ore reserve
steepen the final wall angle
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Not all the waste in the final pit need be moved at once.

**Reasoning.** Dividing the pit into nested phases lets early phases target low-stripping, high-grade ore, deferring deeper waste. This brings revenue forward and reduces the present value of stripping cost, raising NPV.

**Answer.** Defer waste, expose ore early, improve NPV.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: An ore target of 1.2 Mt/yr is mined from a 500 m long, 10 m high working face; ore density is 2.5 t/m³. The required annual face advance, in m/yr, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Advance $=\dfrac{\text{annual ore volume}}{\text{face length}\times\text{bench height}}$.

**Working.**
$$V=\frac{1.2\times10^6}{2.5}=480{,}000\ \text{m}^3/\text{yr}$$
$$\text{advance}=\frac{480{,}000}{500\times10}=96\ \text{m/yr}$$

**Answer.** 96 m/yr.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: If each working face can deliver 240,000 m³/yr of ore and the mine needs 480,000 m³/yr, the number of working faces required is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Faces $=\dfrac{\text{required volume}}{\text{volume per face}}$.

**Working.**
$$N=\frac{480{,}000}{240{,}000}=2$$

**Answer.** 2 faces.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Super-elevation (banking) of a haul-road curve is provided to
options: increase the rolling resistance
counteract lateral (centrifugal) force so trucks can corner safely at speed
drain water into the pit
reduce the grade resistance on straights
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A turning truck experiences an outward centrifugal force.

**Reasoning.** Tilting the road surface inward (super-elevation) resolves part of the truck's weight to balance this force, preventing skidding/rollover and allowing safe cornering.

**Answer.** Counteracts lateral force for safe cornering.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Garland (catch) drains around the rim of an opencast pit are intended to
options: supply water to the haul road for dust suppression
intercept surface runoff and divert it away from the pit
store blasted ore
cool the conveyor belt
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Keeping surface water out reduces pumping load and slope saturation.

**Reasoning.** Garland drains are perimeter channels that catch catchment runoff before it can enter the pit, diverting it to settling ponds, thereby protecting slopes and cutting dewatering cost.

**Answer.** Intercept and divert surface runoff away from the pit.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Groundwater enters a pit sump at 500 m³/h and the pump removes 600 m³/h. The net rate at which the sump is dewatered, in m³/h, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Net dewatering $=$ pumping rate $-$ inflow.

**Working.**
$$\dot V=600-500=100\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Answer.** 100 m³/h.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A pit pump delivers 600 m³/h against a total head of 50 m (water density 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s², efficiency 100%). The hydraulic power, in kW, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Hydraulic power $=\rho g Q H$, with $Q$ in m³/s.

**Working.**
$$Q=\frac{600}{3600}=0.1667\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$P=1000\times9.81\times0.1667\times50=81{,}750\ \text{W}=81.75\ \text{kW}$$

**Answer.** 81.75 kW.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: In the strip-mine cross-section shown, the overburden is 24 m thick over a 6 m coal seam. The in-situ volumetric stripping ratio (m³ overburden per m³ coal) is ____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='0' y='0' width='360' height='220' fill='#ffffff'/><rect x='40' y='40' width='150' height='90' fill='#d9c8a9' stroke='#1f2937'/><rect x='40' y='130' width='150' height='40' fill='#3b3b3b' stroke='#1f2937'/><text x='70' y='90' font-size='12' fill='#111111'>overburden 24 m</text><text x='80' y='154' font-size='11' fill='#ffffff'>coal 6 m</text><line x1='40' y1='40' x2='40' y2='170' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='2' y='110' font-size='11' fill='#dc2626'>highwall</text><polygon points='210,170 330,170 270,70' fill='#cfa86b' stroke='#1f2937'/><text x='255' y='162' font-size='11' fill='#111111'>spoil</text></svg>
figure.caption: Strip-mine section: overburden over a coal seam, highwall on the left and cast spoil on the right.

questions.solution:
**Concept.** For equal areas, the volumetric SR is the thickness ratio.

**Working.**
$$SR=\frac{t_{OB}}{t_{coal}}=\frac{24}{6}=4\ \text{m}^3/\text{m}^3$$

**Answer.** 4.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: For the same section, the overburden density is 2.0 t/m³ and coal density is 1.4 t/m³. The mass stripping ratio (t overburden per t coal) is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Convert each volume to mass using its own density.

**Working.** Per unit plan area: overburden mass $=24\times2.0=48$; coal mass $=6\times1.4=8.4$.
$$SR_{mass}=\frac{48}{8.4}=5.71\ \text{t/t}$$

**Answer.** 5.71 t/t.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: In dragline stripping, 'rehandle' refers to
options: the coal loaded into trucks
spoil that must be moved a second time, reducing the dragline's effective (prime) output
the first dig of fresh overburden
the maintenance time of the dragline
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Limited reach forces some spoil to be cast twice.

**Reasoning.** When the void cannot accept all spoil in one cast, material is placed temporarily and later re-excavated and recast — rehandle — which consumes dragline cycles without removing new overburden, lowering effective production.

**Answer.** Spoil moved a second time, reducing prime output.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A dragline digs a gross 2000 m³/h, of which 20% is rehandle. Its prime (productive) output, in m³/h, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Prime output $=$ gross output $\times(1-\text{rehandle fraction})$.

**Working.**
$$Q_{prime}=2000\times(1-0.20)=1600\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Answer.** 1600 m³/h.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Which of the following are advantages of a walking dragline over a shovel-truck system for thick overburden stripping?
options: Casts spoil directly into the void, eliminating haul trucks
Generally lower stripping cost per m³ for thick overburden
Can selectively mine narrow, high-grade ore veins
Provides continuous belt haulage of ore
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The dragline is a long-reach sidecasting machine.

**Reasoning.** It moves spoil straight into the de-coaled void without trucks and, for thick overburden within its reach, gives a low unit stripping cost. It is not a selective ore miner and does not provide belt haulage.

**Answer.** Direct sidecasting (no trucks) and low unit stripping cost.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The principal limitation of a walking dragline is that
options: it cannot dig soft ground
its fixed reach and dig depth limit the overburden thickness it can strip in one pass
it requires rubber-tyred haulage
it can only work below the water table
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Dragline range is geometrically fixed by boom length.

**Reasoning.** Digging depth, dumping radius and dumping height set a fixed envelope; overburden thicker than this cannot be stripped in a single pass without rehandle or pre-stripping by other equipment.

**Answer.** Fixed reach/dig depth limits overburden thickness.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A dragline is scheduled for all 8760 h in a year, with an availability of 0.85 and a utilization of availability of 0.75. Its effective operating hours per year are ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Operating hours $=$ scheduled $\times$ availability $\times$ utilization.

**Working.**
$$h=8760\times0.85\times0.75=5584.5\ \text{h}$$

**Answer.** 5584.5 h.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: For removing thick, hard, blasted overburden in a deep opencast metal mine, the most suitable prime stripping system is generally a
options: bucket wheel excavator with spreader
large rope shovel with a fleet of rear-dump trucks
fleet of wheeled scrapers
single surface miner
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Equipment must match the ground and the geometry.

**Reasoning.** Hard, blasted rock and a deep, expanding pit rule out continuous diggers (BWE/surface miner — soft ground only) and scrapers (soft, short-haul). A large rope shovel loading rear-dump trucks handles blasted hard rock and flexible deep haul routes.

**Answer.** Large rope shovel with truck haulage.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: For the bench shown (height $H=12$ m, burden $B=4$ m, spacing $S=5$ m), the in-situ volume of rock broken per blasthole, in $\mathrm{m^3}$, is _____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Volume per hole $=B\times S\times H$.

**Working.**
$$V=4\times5\times12=240\ \mathrm{m^3}$$

**Answer.** $240\ \mathrm{m^3}$.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A pit wall (see figure) drops a vertical depth of 45 m over a horizontal run of 60 m from crest to toe. The overall slope angle, in degrees, is _____ (two decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='40' x2='200' y2='40' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='40' x2='80' y2='150' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='20' y1='150' x2='200' y2='150' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='150' x2='200' y2='150' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4 2'/><line x1='200' y1='40' x2='200' y2='150' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4 2'/><text x='120' y='168' font-size='11'>run = 60 m</text><text x='205' y='100' font-size='11'>depth 45 m</text></svg>
figure.caption: Overall pit-wall slope

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Overall slope angle $\psi=\tan^{-1}\!\dfrac{\text{vertical depth}}{\text{horizontal run}}$.

**Working.**
$$\psi=\tan^{-1}\frac{45}{60}=\tan^{-1}(0.75)=36.87^{\circ}$$

**Answer.** $36.87^{\circ}$.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: For the bench shown (height $H=10$ m, burden $B=3.5$ m), the stiffness ratio $H/B$ is _____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stiffness ratio $=\dfrac{H}{B}$; values of 3–4 give good fragmentation, below 2 risk poor breakage.

**Working.**
$$\frac{H}{B}=\frac{10}{3.5}=2.86$$

**Answer.** $2.86$.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: In the cross-section, the overburden area is $300\ \mathrm{m^2}$ and the coal-seam area is $60\ \mathrm{m^2}$ (per metre of strike). The volumetric stripping ratio (overburden : coal) is _____.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 280 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='30' width='220' height='70' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'/><rect x='30' y='100' width='220' height='28' fill='#334155'/><text x='140' y='70' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>Overburden  A = 300 m^2</text><text x='140' y='119' font-size='11' fill='#ffffff' text-anchor='middle'>Coal seam  A = 60 m^2</text></svg>
figure.caption: Overburden and coal cross-section

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stripping ratio $=\dfrac{\text{overburden volume}}{\text{coal volume}}$; per unit strike length this equals the area ratio.

**Working.**
$$SR=\frac{300}{60}=5$$

**Answer.** $5$ (i.e. 5:1).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: For the production bench shown (bench height 12 m, burden 4 m, spacing 5 m), compute the in-situ rock volume broken per blasthole (m³).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Volume broken per hole equals burden × spacing × bench height.

**Working.**
$$V = B \times S \times H = 4 \times 5 \times 12 = 240$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{240}\ \text{m}^3$ per hole.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: An open pit reaches a depth of 60 m and is worked in 15 m high benches as shown. How many benches are required?
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Number of benches = total depth ÷ bench height.

**Working.**
$$N = \frac{60}{15} = 4$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{4}$ benches.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: From the cross-section shown, with overburden cross-sectional area 400 m² and coal cross-sectional area 80 m² per metre of strike, compute the stripping ratio.

figure.caption: Pit cross-section
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='30' width='180' height='70' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#475569'/><text x='120' y='70' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>overburden 400 m^2</text><rect x='30' y='100' width='180' height='30' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='120' y='120' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>coal 80 m^2</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Stripping ratio is overburden volume per unit of coal; per metre strike this is the area ratio.

**Working.**
$$SR = \frac{400}{80} = 5$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{5}$ (5:1).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Using the unit values shown (coal selling price ₹50/t, production cost ₹20/t, stripping cost ₹6 per unit), compute the break-even stripping ratio (BESR).

figure.caption: Unit economics
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='130' x2='200' y2='130' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='50' y='40' width='30' height='90' fill='#16a34a'/><text x='42' y='32' font-size='7'>price 50</text><rect x='100' y='80' width='30' height='50' fill='#f59e0b'/><text x='96' y='72' font-size='7'>cost 20</text><rect x='150' y='110' width='30' height='20' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='144' y='102' font-size='7'>strip 6</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The break-even stripping ratio is the overburden that the net coal value can just pay to remove.

**Working.**
$$BESR = \frac{\text{price} - \text{cost}}{\text{stripping cost}} = \frac{50 - 20}{6} = 5$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{5}$ (mining is economic while actual SR < 5).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A haul ramp descends to a depth of 90 m at a ruling gradient of 9% as shown. Compute the horizontal length (m) of the ramp.

figure.caption: Descending ramp
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='20,30 220,130 20,130' fill='#fde9c8' stroke='#92400e'/><line x1='20' y1='30' x2='20' y2='130' stroke='#475569'/><text x='26' y='80' font-size='8'>depth 90 m</text><text x='100' y='108' font-size='8' transform='rotate(27 100 108)'>grade 9%</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Gradient = rise ÷ horizontal run, so run = rise ÷ gradient.

**Working.**
$$L = \frac{90}{0.09} = 1000$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{1000}$ m of horizontal length.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: From the cycle diagram shown, compute the total truck cycle time (minutes) given load 2, haul 8, dump 1 and return 6 minutes.

figure.caption: Truck cycle
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='110' cy='75' r='50' fill='none' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><text x='110' y='20' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>load 2</text><text x='180' y='78' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>haul 8</text><text x='110' y='140' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>dump 1</text><text x='38' y='78' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>return 6</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Cycle time is the sum of all component times.

**Working.**
$$T_c = 2 + 8 + 1 + 6 = 17$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{17}$ minutes.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A loader serves the fleet shown: 5 trucks, loading time 3 min per truck and truck cycle time 15 min. Compute the match factor.

figure.caption: Loader and truck fleet
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='50' cy='60' r='24' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='50' y='64' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>loader</text><g fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'><rect x='110' y='45' width='28' height='20'/><rect x='150' y='45' width='28' height='20'/><rect x='190' y='45' width='28' height='20'/></g><text x='105' y='90' font-size='8'>5 trucks, load 3 min, cycle 15 min</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Match factor $= \dfrac{N_t \times t_{load}}{T_{cycle}}$; MF = 1 means loader and trucks are balanced.

**Working.**
$$MF = \frac{5 \times 3}{15} = \frac{15}{15} = 1.0$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{1.0}$ (balanced fleet).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A shovel with a 5 m³ dipper has a cycle time of 30 s as shown. Compute its ideal output (loose m³ per hour) assuming a full bucket each cycle.

figure.caption: Shovel dipper
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='40' y='70' width='60' height='30' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='100' y1='80' x2='150' y2='50' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M150 50 q20 0 18 20 l-18 -2 z' fill='#fbbf24' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='52' y='120' font-size='8'>bucket 5 m^3, cycle 30 s</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Output = cycles per hour × bucket volume.

**Working.**
$$Q = \frac{3600}{30} \times 5 = 120 \times 5 = 600$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{600}$ loose m³/h (ideal).

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A truck climbs a 5% grade as shown, with a rolling resistance of 20 kg/t. Taking grade resistance as 10 kg/t per 1% of grade, compute the total resistance (kg/t).

figure.caption: Truck on grade
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='120' x2='220' y2='40' stroke='#92400e' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='120' y='52' width='34' height='18' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569' transform='rotate(-18 137 61)'/><text x='30' y='115' font-size='8'>grade 5%, rolling res 20 kg/t</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Total resistance = rolling resistance + grade resistance.

**Working.**
$$R = 20 + 10 \times 5 = 20 + 50 = 70$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{70}$ kg/t.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: For a fully loaded truck of gross weight 100 t with total resistance 70 kg/t (as shown), compute the rimpull (kg) required to keep it moving up the grade.

figure.caption: Loaded truck on grade
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='120' x2='220' y2='40' stroke='#92400e' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='120' y='52' width='34' height='18' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569' transform='rotate(-18 137 61)'/><text x='28' y='115' font-size='8'>GVW 100 t, total res 70 kg/t</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Required rimpull = total resistance per tonne × gross weight.

**Working.**
$$RP = 70 \times 100 = 7000$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{7000}$ kg.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A belt conveyor carries material of bulk density 1.5 t/m³ at a belt speed of 2 m/s with a load cross-sectional area of 0.1 m² (as shown). Compute the throughput (t/h).

figure.caption: Belt conveyor
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 250 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='40' cy='60' r='18' fill='none' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='210' cy='60' r='18' fill='none' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='42' x2='210' y2='42' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='78' x2='210' y2='78' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M70 42 q20 -14 40 0' fill='#a16207'/><text x='52' y='100' font-size='8'>A=0.1 m^2, v=2 m/s, rho=1.5 t/m^3</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Throughput $Q = 3600 \, A \, v \, \rho$.

**Working.**
$$Q = 3600 \times 0.1 \times 2 \times 1.5 = 1080$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{1080}$ t/h.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A truck of payload 100 t has a cycle time of 15 min (as shown). Compute its hourly output (t/h).

figure.caption: Haul truck
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='40' y='45' width='70' height='30' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='58' cy='80' r='8' fill='#1f2937'/><circle cx='95' cy='80' r='8' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='48' y='40' font-size='8'>payload 100 t</text><text x='44' y='102' font-size='8'>cycle 15 min</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Output = payload × trips per hour, trips/h = 60 ÷ cycle minutes.

**Working.**
$$\frac{60}{15} = 4 \text{ trips/h},\qquad Q = 100 \times 4 = 400$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{400}$ t/h.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: For the pit wall shown with vertical height 60 m and horizontal projection 120 m, compute the overall slope angle (degrees).

figure.caption: Overall pit slope
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='20,30 200,130 20,130' fill='#fde9c8' stroke='#92400e'/><line x1='20' y1='30' x2='20' y2='130' stroke='#475569'/><text x='10' y='84' font-size='8' transform='rotate(-90 12 84)'>H=60</text><text x='92' y='145' font-size='8'>L=120</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The overall slope angle is $\theta = \tan^{-1}(H/L)$.

**Working.**
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{60}{120}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57^\circ$$

**Answer.** $\approx \mathbf{26.57^\circ}$.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: How many truck loads are needed to remove the block shown (4000 m³) with a truck of 20 m³ capacity?

figure.caption: Block to remove
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='40' width='120' height='60' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#475569'/><text x='90' y='75' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>4000 m^3</text><rect x='165' y='60' width='40' height='30' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'/><text x='158' y='110' font-size='8'>truck 20 m^3</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Number of loads = total volume ÷ truck capacity.

**Working.**
$$N = \frac{4000}{20} = 200$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{200}$ loads.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A 100 bank-m³ of rock is excavated. With a swell factor of 1.25 (as shown), compute the loose volume (m³).

figure.caption: Bank vs loose volume
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='60' width='60' height='50' fill='#a8a29e' stroke='#475569'/><text x='60' y='90' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>bank 100</text><rect x='130' y='40' width='60' height='70' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#475569'/><text x='160' y='80' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>loose</text><text x='46' y='125' font-size='8'>swell factor 1.25</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Loose volume = bank volume × swell factor.

**Working.**
$$V_L = 100 \times 1.25 = 125$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{125}$ loose m³.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A 5 m³ bucket digs with a fill factor of 0.9 (as shown). Compute the actual payload per pass (m³).

figure.caption: Bucket fill
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M50 40 L150 40 L135 95 L65 95 Z' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'/><path d='M58 70 L142 70 L135 95 L65 95 Z' fill='#a16207'/><text x='62' y='32' font-size='8'>capacity 5 m^3</text><text x='66' y='112' font-size='8'>fill factor 0.9</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Actual payload = rated capacity × fill factor.

**Working.**
$$V = 5 \times 0.9 = 4.5$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{4.5}$ m³ per pass.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A truck hauls over a one-way distance of 2 km at an average loaded speed of 30 km/h (as shown). Compute the loaded haul time (minutes).

figure.caption: Haul segment
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 100' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='60' x2='220' y2='60' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='20' y='45' width='30' height='18' fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'/><text x='80' y='50' font-size='8'>2 km @ 30 km/h</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Time = distance ÷ speed.

**Working.**
$$t = \frac{2}{30}\,\text{h} = 0.0667\,\text{h} \times 60 = 4\,\text{min}$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{4}$ minutes.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Over the life of mine, 50 Mm³ of overburden must be moved to extract 10 Mt of ore (as shown). Compute the overall (life-of-mine) stripping ratio (m³/t).

figure.caption: Life-of-mine volumes
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='30' width='180' height='70' fill='#d6d3d1' stroke='#475569'/><text x='120' y='70' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>OB 50 Mm^3</text><rect x='30' y='100' width='180' height='30' fill='#1f2937'/><text x='120' y='120' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle' fill='#fff'>ore 10 Mt</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Overall SR = total overburden ÷ total ore.

**Working.**
$$SR = \frac{50}{10} = 5$$

**Answer.** $\mathbf{5}$ m³/t.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: In the plot shown, the actual stripping ratio rises with depth while the break-even stripping ratio (BESR) is constant. The economic pit limit is reached where:

figure.caption: Actual SR vs BESR
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='220' y2='110' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='30' x2='200' y2='30' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><text x='150' y='25' font-size='8' fill='#dc2626'>BESR</text><polyline points='30,100 200,40' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='95' y='128' font-size='8'>actual SR rising with depth</text></svg>

options: the actual stripping ratio equals the BESR
the actual stripping ratio is zero
the pit floor is horizontal
the BESR exceeds the selling price
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Mining is profitable while actual SR < BESR.

**Reasoning.** As the pit deepens the actual SR increases; the last economic increment is where it just reaches the BESR. Beyond that, removing overburden costs more than the coal it exposes is worth.

**Answer.** Where the actual stripping ratio equals the BESR.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The maximum stable slope of the loose spoil dump shown is governed primarily by:

figure.caption: Spoil dump
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='40,110 140,110 90,50' fill='#d6c0a3' stroke='#92400e'/><path d='M90 50 A50 50 0 0 0 40 110' fill='none' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='92' y='98' font-size='8'>repose angle</text></svg>

options: the angle of repose of the material
the bench height
the truck payload
the conveyor speed
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A free-standing pile of loose material stands at its angle of repose.

**Reasoning.** The dump face angle cannot exceed the angle of repose of the spoil; this friction-controlled angle governs maximum stable dump slope.

**Answer.** The angle of repose of the material.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The machine shown sits on top of the bench and casts overburden directly to the spoil side. It is a:

figure.caption: Stripping machine
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='30' y='70' width='50' height='30' fill='#94a3b8' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='70' y1='80' x2='180' y2='30' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='180' y1='30' x2='170' y2='95' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><path d='M160 95 q12 6 20 -4 l-8 -8 z' fill='#fbbf24' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='44' y='125' font-size='8'>casting overburden to spoil</text></svg>

options: walking dragline
hydraulic backhoe truck-loading shovel
wheel loader
scraper
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A dragline works from the highwall top and casts overburden to the spoil side without trucks.

**Reasoning.** The long boom and drag-bucket casting spoil directly across the cut is characteristic of a walking dragline used in strip mining.

**Answer.** Walking dragline.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: For the loader–truck arrangement shown the match factor is less than 1. This means:

figure.caption: Under-trucked loader
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='50' cy='60' r='24' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#b45309'/><text x='50' y='64' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>loader</text><g fill='#bfdbfe' stroke='#475569'><rect x='120' y='45' width='28' height='20'/></g><text x='100' y='95' font-size='8'>too few trucks (MF < 1)</text></svg>

options: the loader waits idle for trucks (loader under-utilised)
the trucks queue and wait for the loader
both loader and trucks are fully utilised
the haul road gradient is too steep
questions.solution:
**Concept.** MF < 1 means truck arrival capacity is below loader capacity.

**Reasoning.** With too few trucks the loader finishes loading and must wait for the next truck, so the loader is the under-utilised (idle) unit. MF > 1 would instead cause trucks to queue.

**Answer.** The loader waits idle for trucks.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: The green horizontal strip left on the pit wall in the figure is a catch berm. Its main purpose is to:

figure.caption: Catch berm on pit wall
